worth_it_explanation: Curation packages how a publisher's inventory and data reach buyers, so a deal that targets the wrong inventory, or a package that no longer matches the audience it claims to carry, sells the wrong thing at the wrong price. The check is a review of curated deal and audience packages a publisher already has in Equativ, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete packaging decision the publisher can correct.
intro: Curation lets a publisher package inventory and audience data into deals that buyers can target directly, rather than leaving every impression to the open auction. In Equativ, formerly Smart AdServer, those curated packages carry a deal identifier (deal ID) that maps the package to specific inventory and, in many cases, to an audience segment built from the publisher's first-party data. This subcategory is about whether those packages are set up correctly and kept current. These checks confirm that each curated deal points at the inventory and audience it claims to, and that packages are maintained rather than left to drift as inventory and audiences change.

audit_checks.title: Curated deal and audience packages are not mapped to the right inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package sells a buyer a specific promise, namely a defined slice of inventory and often a named audience. If the deal ID maps to the wrong ad units, or the audience segment behind the package does not match the data it claims, the buyer pays a curated price for inventory that does not match the package. That breaks the deal's value and can cost the publisher the relationship when the buyer reads the delivery.

educate.base:
Curation turns raw inventory into a packaged offer. A publisher selects a slice of inventory, often layers an audience segment built from first-party data on top, and exposes the package to buyers through a deal identifier (deal ID). The buyer targets that deal ID in their own buying platform, and Equativ, formerly Smart AdServer, matches the bid against the inventory and audience the package defines. The package is a promise, so the buyer is paying a curated price on the understanding that the deal delivers the inventory and audience it describes.

The promise holds only when the mapping behind it is correct. Two things have to line up. First, the deal ID has to point at the inventory the package claims, meaning the right ad units, formats, and any geographic or contextual targeting the package names. A deal sold as premium homepage video that maps to a run-of-site display package delivers something the buyer did not agree to buy. Second, where the package carries an audience, the segment behind it has to match the data the package claims to carry. An audience package described as in-market shoppers that maps to a stale or mislabeled segment sells a story the data does not support.

Equativ packages curated audiences using the publisher's first-party data, and in a Prebid-compatible setup that audience data travels through the standard OpenRTB first-party data fields (site and user data objects) so buyers can target against it. Because Equativ is Prebid-compatible, the mechanics of how a deal ID and audience segment reach a buyer follow the same patterns the Prebid documentation describes for deals and first-party data. The audit question is whether each curated package's deal ID and audience segment resolve to the inventory and data the package was sold on, not to something that drifted out of alignment.

A few signs point to packages that may be mapped to the wrong inventory:
  • A deal ID's targeting in Equativ covers ad units, formats, or geographies that do not match how the package is described to buyers
  • An audience package references a first-party segment whose definition or freshness no longer matches the audience name the buyer sees
  • Two or more packages share overlapping inventory or audience definitions in ways that make it unclear which one a buyer is actually targeting
You may want to confirm that each curated package's deal ID and audience segment resolve to the inventory and data the package claims. One approach is to walk each active curated deal from its buyer-facing description back to the inventory targeting and audience segment behind it, and record any case where the two do not match.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, curated packages often sell a section or a content context, for example a sports vertical or a recipe audience, so the mapping has to tie the deal ID to the ad units and content categories that actually carry that context. You may want to confirm that a package sold on a content theme maps to the inventory in that section rather than to run-of-site, which would dilute the package the buyer paid for.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, curated audience packages may sell intent signals such as recent product or category viewers, so the audience segment behind the package has to reflect the first-party data that defines that intent. You may want to confirm that an intent-based package maps to the segment built from real on-site behavior rather than a broad or outdated audience that does not carry the intent the buyer is paying for.

investigate.intro: Review each active curated deal in Equativ from its buyer-facing description back to the inventory targeting and audience segment behind it, and record any case where the two do not match.

equativ_ui.label: Equativ curation platform
steps: Open the curation workspace and list the active curated deals, noting each deal's identifier (deal ID) and the description buyers see.
For each deal, open its inventory targeting and record the ad units, formats, and any geographic or contextual targeting it covers.
Where a deal carries an audience, open the segment behind it and record its definition and the first-party data source it draws from.
Compare the deal's buyer-facing description against the inventory targeting and audience segment, and record any deal where the description and the underlying mapping do not match.
Note any two packages whose inventory or audience definitions overlap in a way that makes the targeting ambiguous.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per curated deal, the buyer-facing description, the inventory targeting behind the deal ID, and the audience segment where one applies. A deal whose inventory targeting and audience segment match the description buyers see passes. A deal whose targeting or segment does not match the description, or whose definition overlaps another package in a way that makes the targeting ambiguous, is a fail recorded with the deal ID and the mismatch found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the description, inventory targeting, and audience segment for each curated deal above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more curated packages on {{site_name}} map to inventory or an audience segment that does not match the package description buyers see. A curated deal is a promise, so when the deal identifier (deal ID) points at the wrong ad units or the audience segment no longer matches the data the package claims, the buyer pays a curated price for something other than what they agreed to buy. {{voice_recommend}} walking each active curated deal from its buyer-facing description back to the inventory targeting and audience segment behind it, and correcting any deal where the two do not line up. After the change, recheck the corrected deals to confirm the deal ID and segment resolve to the inventory and data the package was sold on.

audit_checks.title: Curated packages are not maintained and drift out of date
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Inventory and audiences change, so a curated package that was accurate when it launched can drift as ad units are retired, sections are restructured, and first-party segments age. A stale package keeps selling against a definition the inventory and data no longer support, which erodes delivery and buyer trust until a buyer notices the package no longer performs as described.

educate.base:
A curated package is set once but lives against inventory and audiences that keep changing. Ad units are added and retired, sections are restructured, formats are swapped, and first-party audience segments age as the behavior that defined them recedes. A package that described the inventory and audience accurately on the day it launched can drift away from that description over the months that follow, while the deal stays live and keeps selling against the original promise.

The drift is rarely visible from inside the package because the deal identifier (deal ID) and the segment definition do not change on their own. What changes is the world they point at. An audience segment built on a thirty-day window of product viewers shrinks and refreshes constantly, so a package that names that audience is only as current as the segment behind it. A package tied to a content section that has since been folded into another section now targets inventory that no longer exists under the old structure. The package looks healthy in the deal list while the thing it sells has moved.

Equativ packages curated audiences from a publisher's first-party data, and in a Prebid-compatible setup that data flows through the standard OpenRTB first-party data fields, where a segment is only as good as the data feeding it. Because Equativ is Prebid-compatible, the freshness of an audience segment depends on the same first-party data pipeline the Prebid documentation describes, and a segment that stops refreshing carries an audience that ages out. The audit question is whether the publisher reviews curated packages on a schedule and retires or refreshes the ones that no longer match current inventory and data, rather than leaving them to drift.

A few signs point to packages that are not maintained:
  • Curated deals have been live for many months with no recorded review of their inventory targeting or audience definition
  • Audience segments behind packages show shrinking size or stale refresh dates that suggest the underlying data has aged
  • Packages reference ad units, sections, or formats that have since been retired or restructured
  • No owner or schedule exists for reviewing and retiring curated packages
You may want to confirm that curated packages are reviewed on a schedule and retired or refreshed when they no longer match current inventory and data. One approach is to read the launch date, last review date, and current segment size for each active package, then flag the ones that have gone unreviewed or whose audience has aged.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, content sections and editorial themes change as coverage shifts, so a package tied to a section or theme can outlive the structure it was built on. You may want to review curated packages against the current section structure and retire any that reference sections or themes the site no longer organizes content around.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, intent-based audience segments age quickly because the on-site behavior that defines them refreshes constantly, so a package selling recent shoppers depends on a segment that has to stay current. You may want to check the refresh date and size of each intent segment behind a package and refresh or retire the ones whose audience has aged out of the window the package claims.

learn_more.source_title: First Party Data in Prebid Server

investigate.intro: Review the launch date, last review date, and current audience segment size for each active curated package, and flag the ones that have gone unreviewed or whose inventory or audience has aged.

equativ_ui.label: Equativ curation platform
steps: Open the curation workspace and list the active curated deals with their launch dates.
For each deal, record when its inventory targeting and audience definition were last reviewed, if a review date is tracked.
Where a deal carries an audience, open the segment and record its current size and last refresh date.
Check each deal's inventory targeting against the current ad unit and section structure, and note any that reference retired or restructured inventory.
Record whether an owner and a review schedule exist for the curated packages, or whether packages are left live without a maintenance routine.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per curated package, its launch date, last review date, current audience segment size and refresh date where one applies, and whether its inventory targeting matches the current structure. Packages reviewed on a schedule, with segments that refresh and inventory targeting that matches current ad units and sections, pass. Packages left unreviewed for many months, with aged segments or targeting that references retired inventory, are a fail recorded with the package and the drift found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the launch date, last review date, and segment size for each curated package above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that curated packages on {{site_name}} are left live without a maintenance routine, so some have drifted out of date as inventory and audiences changed. A package that described its inventory and audience accurately at launch can age as ad units are retired, sections are restructured, and first-party segments shrink, while the deal keeps selling against the original promise. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing curated packages on a schedule, reading the launch date, last review date, and current segment freshness for each, and retiring or refreshing the packages that no longer match current inventory and data. After setting the routine, assign an owner so packages are reviewed before they drift rather than after a buyer notices.
